source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 6.1 & 6.6.5 saving and quitting (pp. 84, 95)

questions.q: In ex mode, quit VI WITHOUT saving (the file has not been modified).
questions.why: :q quits; the 'q' stands for quit. It refuses if there are unsaved changes.

questions.q: In ex mode, save the file (write) without quitting.
questions.why: :w writes (saves) the buffer to the file; 'w' stands for write.

questions.q: In ex mode, save AND quit in one command (give either standard form).
questions.why: :wq writes then quits; :x is the equivalent shorthand (eXit).

questions.q: From command mode (not ex mode), which key pressed twice saves and exits?
questions.why: Pressing uppercase Z twice (ZZ) saves and exits directly from command mode.

questions.q: You made changes but want to quit WITHOUT saving them; force VI to discard the modifications and quit.
questions.why: Plain :q refuses to quit a modified buffer; adding ! forces the exit without confirmation, discarding the changes.

questions.q: What does adding '!' to a quit or write command (e.g. :q! or :w!) do?
options: Runs it as a shell command
questions.why: The ! forces the command through without asking for confirmation - for example discarding unsaved changes with :q!.

questions.q: Save the current buffer under a different name, notes.txt.
questions.why: :w <file> writes the buffer to a new file name, leaving the original untouched.
